bull.t: High Product Gross Margins / Unit Economics
bull.b: Very high product-level gross margins and favorable unit economics create durable upside if commercial scale is achieved. Low COGS per pack means incremental sales can drop to the bottom line quickly, supporting long-term profitability potential once fixed commercial costs normalize.

bull.t: Growing Prescriber Base And Script Traction
bull.b: A broad and expanding prescriber base with improving repeat prescribing signals durable commercial penetration. Higher scripts per prescriber versus peer launches indicates stronger adoption economics and provides a foundation to scale repeat sales without linear increases in field costs.

A strong cash balance and negligible debt reduce near-term insolvency risk and give management flexibility to fund commercialization, manufacturing transitions, and promotional programs. This financial cushion supports multi-quarter execution without immediate refinancing pressure.

bear.b: Persistent negative operating and free cash flow indicates the business is consuming capital rather than generating it. Until revenue and refill rates scale, the company will remain reliant on external financing, which can dilute equity and constrain strategic optionality over the medium term.

bear.t: High SG&A And Large Operating Losses
bear.b: Front-loaded commercial spend and elevated SG&A drive sizable operating losses that can persist if marketing fails to convert. High fixed selling costs lengthen the path to breakeven and increase sensitivity to demand shortfalls, pressuring capital runway and returns.

bear.t: Uncertainty In Refill Persistence And Adoption Durability
bear.b: Lack of mature cohort refill data and slower new-patient starts create structural uncertainty about recurring revenue. If refill rates or physician adoption remain muted, durable sales growth may require prolonged promotional spend and reduce lifetime value per patient.
